title: Interpreting graphs of the production possibilities curve (PPC)
description: Practice what you have learned about the production possibilities curve (PPC) in this exercise.
topic_path: Interpreting graphs of the production possibilities curve (PPC)

assessment_items.question_content:
The graph below represents a change in the production possibilities curve (PPC) for the nation of Xam.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following statements about the change shown in this PPC are true?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A graph illustrates a production possibilities frontier (P P F) with two axes: Fast Cars on the horizontal axis ranging from 0 to 14 and Fruit Smoothies on the vertical axis ranging from 0 to 8. Two curves are depicted, P P C 1 and P P C 2, representing different levels of production capacity. P P C 2 is shifted outward from P P C 1. The curve for P P C 1 decreases from (0, 8) to (9, 0). The curve for P P C 2 decreases from (0, 8) to (15, 0). Key points are marked on the graph. Point W at (0, 8) lies on both P P C 1 and P P C 2. Point X at (15, 0) lies on both P P C 1 and P P C 2. Point Y at (9, 0) lies on P P C 1. Point V at (12, 5) lies on P P C 2. Point Z at (7, 5) lies on P P C 1. Point U at (5, 3). An arrow points from P P C 1 to P P C 2, illustrating the outward shift of the curve.
options.caption: Xam's production possibilities

choices.content: Xam now has more resources or technology available that can produce either fast cars or fruit smoothies
Xam now has more resources or technology available to produce more fast cars, but not fruit smoothies
Xam now has more resources or technology to produce more fruit smoothies, but not fast cars
Xam has fewer resources or technology to produce fruit smoothies and fast cars
Xam's resources or technology have not changed

assessment_items.question_content:
A company can use its current resources to produce hats and boots as shown in this production possibilities curve (PPC).

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following represents a movement from inefficiency to efficiency in this PPC?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A line graph depicts a production possibilities curve labeled P P C 1, representing the trade-off between the production of hats and an unspecified second good. The graph has a vertical axis labeled Hats and a horizontal axis labeled Quantity. The curve P P C 1 is downward-sloping and concave, starting at point V at (0, 8) on the Y-axis and ending at point R at (9, 0) on the X-axis. Points are marked along and within the curve. Five points are marked on the graph. Point V is at (0, 8) on the P P C 1 curve. Point R is at (10, 0) on the P P C 1 curve. Point S is at approximately (7, 5) on the P P C 1 curve. Point T is at approximately (5, 3) inside the P P C 1 curve. Point U is at approximately (9, 6) outside the P P C 1 curve.

choices.content: From T to S
From S to R
From S to V
From S to U
From V to T

assessment_items.question_content:
The figure below illustrates the production possibilities curve for a restaurant making kolaches and bowls of queso.
 
[[☃ image 1]]


**Given the production possibilities curve (PPC) shown here, which of the following represents a movement from efficiency to inefficiency?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: The figure represents a production possibility curve in the first quadrant of a coordinate plane. The horizontal axis is labeled queso open parentheses bowls closed parentheses and the vertical axis is labeled kolaches. The bowed out  P P C curve is labeled P P C and begins on the vertical axis at the coordinates 0 and 8 and extends gradually downward and to the right until it ends on the horizontal axis at the coordinates 9 and 0. There are 3 points on the P P C curve: a point labeled F is indicated on the P P C curve at the coordinates 0 and 8, a point labeled G on the P P C curve at the coordinates 7 and 5, and a point labeled I at the coordinates 9 and 0. There is a point labeled H that lies beneath the P P C curve at the coordinates 5 and 3. There is a point labeled J that lies above the P P C curve at coordinates 9 and 6.

choices.content: From G to H
From G to I
From J to G
H to F
From G to F

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of these graphs shows what happens when the technology used to produce queso, but not kolaches, improves?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: ![A line graph shows a shift in a production possibilities curve, illustrating the trade-off between the production of Kolaches and Queso. The graph has a vertical axis labeled Kolaches and a horizontal axis labeled Queso. Two curves are displayed. The initial curve, labeled P P C 1, is concave and starts on the Y-axis and ends on the X-axis. The second curve, labeled P P C 2, is also concave, shifted outward from P P C 1. An arrow pointing from P P C 1 to P P C 2 illustrates the outward shift.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/46254879ab5cd351edd32a82c1490bbe77303a6d)

![A line graph shows a shift in a production possibilities curve, illustrating the trade-off between the production of Kolaches and Queso. The graph has a vertical axis labeled Kolaches and a horizontal axis labeled Queso. Two curves are displayed. The initial curve, labeled P P C 1, is concave and starts on the Y-axis and ends on the X-axis. The second curve, labeled P P C 2, is also concave, shifted outward from P P C 1. An arrow pointing from P P C 1 to P P C 2 illustrates the outward shift.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/f7141f9b4de645b983bb3eae5fa4738f05f03acb)

![A line graph shows a shift in a production possibilities curve, illustrating the trade-off between the production of Kolaches and Queso. The graph has a vertical axis labeled Kolaches and a horizontal axis labeled Queso. Two curves are displayed. The initial curve, labeled P P C 1, is concave and starts on the Y-axis and ends on the X-axis. The second curve, labeled P P C 2, is also concave, shifted outward from P P C 1. Both the curves end on the same point on the x-axis. An arrow pointing from P P C 1 to P P C 2 illustrates the outward shift.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/328c28382dc18dd282b2fbbf5bfc6f47bc9eba04)

![A line graph shows a shift in a production possibilities curve, illustrating the trade-off between the production of Kolaches and Queso. The graph has a vertical axis labeled Kolaches and a horizontal axis labeled Queso. Two curves are displayed. The initial curve, labeled P P C 1, is concave and starts on the Y-axis and ends on the X-axis. The second curve, labeled P P C 2, is also concave, shifted inward from P P C 1. An arrow pointing from P P C 1 to P P C 2 illustrates the inward shift.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/51a70ca09f076d081d9065d97ddc2788f7d41fc5)

![A line graph depicts a production possibilities curve labeled P P C. The vertical axis is labeled as Kolaches. The horizontal axis is labeled as Queso. The P P C curve is concave and downward-sloping, starting at the top left and ending at the bottom right. Two points are marked on the curve. An arrow extends from the upper point on the curve to the lower point on the curve.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a3d2430e7e6571fcac0f67b17bbdce3e85e5cd6f)

assessment_items.question_content:
The graph below shows the PPC for a country producing ice cream and frozen yogurt. 

[[☃ image 1]]

**What statement explains why the PPC for ice cream and frozen yogurt is a straight line instead of a curve?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A line graph shows a linear production possibilities curve for Ice Cream and Frozen Yogurt. The vertical axis, labeled Ice Cream (gallons), ranges from 0 to 10 with increments of 1. The horizontal axis, labeled Frozen Yogurt (gallons), ranges from 0 to 10 with increments of 1. The curve is a straight downward-sloping line connecting the point (0, 10) on the Y-axis to the point (10, 0) on the X-axis.
options.caption: A production possibilities curve

choices.content: The shape of the PPC signifies that the resources needed to produce the two goods are interchangeable.
The shape of the PPC signifies that the resources needed to produce the two goods are very different.
The shape of the PPC signifies increasing opportunity cost
The shape of the PPC signifies an inefficient use of resources
The shape of the PPC signifies efficient use of resources

assessment_items.question_content:
This graph shows the PPC for a country producing ice cream and scooters. Study the graph and answer the question that follows.

[[☃ image 2]]

**Which of the following best explains why this PPC is bowed outward from the origin?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A line graph on a coordinate plane. The vertical axis, labeled Ice Cream (gallons), ranges from 0 to 9 with increments of 1. The horizontal axis, labeled Scooters, ranges from 0 to 9 with increments of 1. The curve decreases from (0, 8) on the y-axis to (9, 0) on the X-axis.
options.caption: A production possibilities curve

choices.content: The resources used to produce scooters and ice cream are equally suited to producing scooters and ice cream.
The production of scooters and ice cream is inefficient.
The resources used to produce scooters and ice cream are not interchangeable.
The opportunity cost of increasing production of scooters or ice cream is constant at any level of production.
Production of scooters and ice cream is efficient.

assessment_items.question_content:
The graph here shows the production possibilities of two goods, R and S. 

[[☃ image 1]]

**How does the opportunity cost of Good S change as output of S increases?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A line graph on a coordinate plane. The vertical axis, labeled Good R, ranges from 0 to 10 with increments of 1. The horizontal axis, labeled Good S, ranges from 0 to 10 with increments of 1. The curve decreases from (0, 10) on the y-axis to (10, 0) on the X-axis.

choices.content: It decreases
It increases
It is constant
It is zero
It becomes negative

assessment_items.question_content:
The nation of Eoth can produce two goods: Good L and good M. The production possibilities curve for Eoth is shown here.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What does the shape of the PPC say about the production of these two goods?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A line graph on a coordinate plane. The vertical axis, labeled Good L, ranges from 0 to 10 with increments of 1. The horizontal axis, labeled Good M, ranges from 0 to 10 with increments of 1. The curve decreases from (0, 8) on the y-axis to (9, 0) on the X-axis.
options.caption: Production possibilities for Eoth

choices.content: Goods L and M require very different resources to produce.
There are decreasing opportunity costs as more M is produced.
Goods L and M require identical resources to produce.
There is zero opportunity cost in terms of Good L given up as output of Good M increases.
Goods L and M are used in fixed proportions to produce another good.

assessment_items.question_content:
[[☃ image 1]]

The PPC shown is bowed out from the origin. 

**What does the shape of this PPC indicate about how opportunity cost changes as more of one good is produced?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A line graph on a coordinate plane. The vertical axis, labeled Ice Cream (gallons), ranges from 0 to 9 with increments of 1. The horizontal axis, labeled Scooters, ranges from 0 to 9 with increments of 1. The curve decreases from (0, 8) on the y-axis to (9, 0) on the X-axis.
options.caption: A production possibilities curve

choices.content: Increasing opportunity cost
Decreasing opportunity cost
Constant opportunity cost
Zero opportunity cost
The PPC does not provide information about opportunity costs

assessment_items.question_content:
Kitt’s production possibilities curve (PPC) is shown in this graph.


![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/41090c54b946c6a7a95425e29423d3388836c761)

**Which of the following is true based on the PPC shown here?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The opportunity cost of writing an article or designing a logo remains constant as the production of the other good increases.
Kitt’s opportunity cost of writing another article in terms of designing a new logo increases as more articles are written.
Kitt’s opportunity cost of writing another article in terms of designing a new logo decreases as more articles are written.
Kitt’s opportunity cost of designing a new logo in terms of writing another article increases as more logos are designed.
Kitt’s opportunity cost of designing a new logo in terms of writing another article decreases as more logos are designed.

assessment_items.question_content:
Kitt’s production possibilities curve (PPC) is shown in this graph.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9286a050f23f13f75fef1a8a5cdf165ed4fe3ec9)

**Based on a comparison of points X, W, Y, and Z, which of the following is correct about the opportunity cost of writing an article?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It is the same at all points
It is lowest at point Z
It is highest at point X
It decreases as you move from X to Z
It increases as you move from Y to W

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following would NOT be part of the opportunity costs of going to college?**

[[☃ radio 2]]

choices.content: The money spent on tuition
Interest payments on student loans
Money spent on textbooks
Foregone wages given up to attend college
Money spent on clothes

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following statements best describes opportunity costs?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The price to a consumer of a good or service
The tradeoffs faced when choosing how to use a scarce resource
The value of things given up when a decision is made.
The monetary cost of any economic transaction
The cost a buyer sees for something they want

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following concepts can be modeled using a PPC?**
[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: When all resources are being used, making more of one good means making less of the other good.
Societies have preferences about what should be produced
Prices that the goods will sell for will be determined in a market
Governments, rather than markets, are more efficient allocators of resources
A society can produce more of any good just by reallocating its resources more efficiently

assessment_items.question_content:
Alex can split his time doing chores between only two activities: folding laundry and cooking meals. His production possibilities curve is shown here.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Based on Alex's PPC, which of the following statements best describes what we can say about how he should split his time?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A line graph depicts a production possibilities curve showing the relationship between Folding Laundry (baskets) on the vertical axis and Meals Cooked on the horizontal axis. The vertical axis ranges from 0 to 6 with increments of 1, and the horizontal axis ranges from 0 to 11 with increments of 1. The production possibilities curve is labeled as a downward-sloping curve, starting at point D at (0, 4) and ending at point A at (9, 0). Five points are marked on the graph. Point D at (0, 4) is on the curve. Point A at (9, 0) is on the curve. Point E at approximately (6, 3) is on the curve. Point B at approximately (2, 2) is inside the curve. Point C at approximately (10, 5) is outside the curve.
options.caption: A PPC with points illustrating different combinations of production

choices.content: We cannot tell how he should split his time
He should produce at point $B$ because he has time left over to do other chores
He should produce the combination at point $E$ because he splits his time between the two activities
He should produce the combination at point $C$ because it represents the most laundry folded and meals cooked
He should produce at point $A$ because food is a need but laundry is not

assessment_items.question_content:
The graph below represents a change in the production possibilities of the nation of Xam.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following statements about the economic growth in this PPC are true?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A line graph shows two production possibilities curves, labeled P P C 1 and P P C 2, depicting the trade-off between Fruit Smoothies on the vertical axis and an unspecified second good on the horizontal axis. The vertical axis ranges from 0 to 8 with increments of 2, and the horizontal axis ranges from 0 to 14 with increments of 2. The curve labeled P P C 1 starts at point W at (0, 8) and ends at point Y at (9, 0). The curve labeled P P C 2 starts at point W at (0, 8) and ends at point X at (15, 0), shifted outward from P P C 1. Five points are marked. Point W is at (0, 8), on both P P C 1 and P P C 2. Point Y is at (9, 0) on P P C 1. Point X is at (15, 0) on P P C 2. Point Z is at approximately (7, 5) on P P C 1. Point V is at approximately (12, 5) on P P C 2. Point U is at approximately (5, 3) inside P P C 1. An arrow points from P P C 1 to P P C 2.
options.caption: Xam's production possibilities

choices.content: Xam now has more resources or technology available that can produce either fast cars or fruit smoothies
Xam now has more resources or technology available to produce more fast cars, but not fruit smoothies
Xam now has more resources or technology to produce more fruit smoothies, but not fast cars
Xam has fewer resources or technology to produce fruit smoothies and fast cars
Xam's resources or technology have not changed

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is an accurate representation of economic growth?**

[[☃ radio 2]]

choices.content: Any increase in output of goods or services when an economy starts at a point inside its PPC
An increase in capital available without any increase in production
An increase in both the potential and actual level of output of goods and services over time
An increase in human resources that aren't used
An increase in the amount of money circulating in the economy

assessment_items.question_content:
Flour is an ingredient used to make kolaches, but is not used to make queso.

**Which of these graphs shows what happens when the availability of flour increases?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: ![A line graph shows a shift in a production possibilities curve, illustrating the trade-off between the production of Kolaches and Queso. The graph has a vertical axis labeled Kolaches and a horizontal axis labeled Queso. Two curves are displayed. The initial curve, labeled P P C 1, is concave and starts on the Y-axis and ends on the X-axis. The second curve, labeled P P C 2, is also concave, shifted outward from P P C 1. Both the curves end on the same point on the x-axis. An arrow pointing from P P C 1 to P P C 2 illustrates the outward shift.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/328c28382dc18dd282b2fbbf5bfc6f47bc9eba04)

![A line graph shows a shift in a production possibilities curve, illustrating the trade-off between the production of Kolaches and Queso. The graph has a vertical axis labeled Kolaches and a horizontal axis labeled Queso. Two curves are displayed. The initial curve, labeled P P C 1, is concave and starts on the Y-axis and ends on the X-axis. The second curve, labeled P P C 2, is also concave, shifted outward from P P C 1. An arrow pointing from P P C 1 to P P C 2 illustrates the outward shift.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/46254879ab5cd351edd32a82c1490bbe77303a6d)

![A line graph shows a shift in a production possibilities curve, illustrating the trade-off between the production of Kolaches and Queso. The graph has a vertical axis labeled Kolaches and a horizontal axis labeled Queso. Two curves are displayed. The initial curve, labeled P P C 1, is concave and starts on the Y-axis and ends on the X-axis. The second curve, labeled P P C 2, is also concave, shifted outward from P P C 1. An arrow pointing from P P C 1 to P P C 2 illustrates the outward shift.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/f7141f9b4de645b983bb3eae5fa4738f05f03acb)

![A line graph shows a shift in a production possibilities curve, illustrating the trade-off between the production of Kolaches and Queso. The graph has a vertical axis labeled Kolaches and a horizontal axis labeled Queso. Two curves are displayed. The initial curve, labeled P P C 1, is concave and starts on the Y-axis and ends on the X-axis. The second curve, labeled P P C 2, is also concave, shifted inward from P P C 1. An arrow pointing from P P C 1 to P P C 2 illustrates the inward shift.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/51a70ca09f076d081d9065d97ddc2788f7d41fc5)

![A line graph depicts a production possibilities curve labeled P P C. The vertical axis is labeled as Kolaches. The horizontal axis is labeled as Queso. The P P C curve is concave and downward-sloping, starting at the top left and ending at the bottom right. Two points are marked on the curve. An arrow extends from the upper point on the curve to the lower point on the curve.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a3d2430e7e6571fcac0f67b17bbdce3e85e5cd6f)

assessment_items.question_content:
The graph below illustrates two production possibilities of the country of Xam. 

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following movements would represent economic growth?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A line graph shows two production possibilities curves, labeled P P C 1 and P P C 2, depicting the trade-off between Fruit Smoothies on the vertical axis and an unspecified second good on the horizontal axis. The vertical axis ranges from 0 to 8 with increments of 2, and the horizontal axis ranges from 0 to 14 with increments of 2. The curve labeled P P C 1 starts at point W at (0, 8) and ends at point Y at (9, 0). The curve labeled P P C 2 starts at point W at (0, 8) and ends at point X at (15, 0), shifted outward from P P C 1. Five points are marked. Point W is at (0, 8), on both P P C 1 and P P C 2. Point Y is at (9, 0) on P P C 1. Point X is at (15, 0) on P P C 2. Point Z is at approximately (7, 5) on P P C 1. Point V is at approximately (12, 5) on P P C 2. Point U is at approximately (5, 3) inside P P C 1. An arrow points from P P C 1 to P P C 2.
options.caption: Xam's production possibilities

choices.content: From $U$ to $Z$
From $U$ to $Y$
From $U$ to $W$
From $Z$ to $V$
$X$ to $Z$
